chapters.title: cold open
chapters.scriptText:
It's just past three in the morning and an employee at Stryker Corporation's Irish hub reaches for their phone on the nightstand and finds it halfway through a factory reset. They haven't touched it — they went to bed with a full battery and a lock screen, and now there's a progress bar moving steadily toward the end, and there is nothing they can do about it because by the time you're reading a factory reset in progress, it's already been authorised somewhere else, on a server you'll never reach in time.
They get up and go to the kitchen where the laptop is open on the table, and the screen is doing the same thing.
They are a senior IT administrator at one of the largest medical technology companies in the world, and they know exactly what this means — not just for their own devices, but for every device connected to the same management system, in every office, in every country. They grab a phone that isn't enrolled in the corporate network and start making calls.
By sunrise, across nearly eighty countries — on surgical tablets in Michigan, on administrator consoles in Singapore, on the personal phones of thousands of employees who had simply connected them to the work network and never thought about it again — two hundred thousand devices had been wiped clean, with no ransom note, no warning, and no countdown timer, just a single command issued in the middle of the night by someone who had stolen the one credential that had power over all of it.
When markets opened that morning, Stryker's stock dropped nearly four percent within hours, and kept falling — shedding roughly eight billion dollars in value over the days that followed.
This is the story of the most surgical corporate cyberattack ever documented — a wiper attack conducted entirely without a wiper, beginning not with a line of malicious code but with a stolen key and a door that should never have opened from the outside. Brian Krebs broke this story, and everything you're about to hear is documented — sources and technical references are in the description.

chapters.title: PART ONE — THE COMPANY IN THE DARK
chapters.scriptText:
To understand why this story matters — why this attack sent emergency memos racing through hospital networks and forced paramedics to revert to radio calls for cardiac patients — you need to understand who Stryker is.
Fifty-six thousand employees. Sixty-one countries. Twenty-five billion dollars in annual revenue. Their products include the surgical robots used in hip replacements, the powered stretchers in emergency rooms, the orthopedic implants that restore mobility to patients who had none, and LifeNet — a cardiac transmission platform that lets paramedics beam a patient's electrocardiogram to a waiting emergency physician before the ambulance has even reached the hospital door.
Almost every hospital in the United States that performs surgery sources equipment through Stryker. They are not background. They are the foundation.
On the morning of the attack, all of it went dark at once.
More than five thousand employees at Stryker's largest hub outside the United States were sent home. Manufacturing lines stood motionless. Staff were communicating through WhatsApp on personal phones because every official channel had been destroyed. When a journalist called the company's main US headquarters, the voicemail said simply: 'We are currently experiencing a building emergency. Please try your call again later.'
In Maryland, the state's emergency medical services office sent a memo to paramedics across the state. Stryker's LifeNet cardiac platform was unavailable. If they could not transmit a twelve-lead ECG to a receiving hospital, they should initiate radio consultation and describe the findings verbally.
The implication was immediate: cardiac patients in ambulances, their readings described over radio rather than transmitted automatically — because the system that was supposed to do it was offline.
To understand how this was possible, you need to understand a tool most people have never heard of — and a technique that leaves almost no fingerprints.

chapters.title: PART TWO — THE GOD VIEW
chapters.scriptText:
In the modern enterprise, the old idea of a secure perimeter is dead. Employees work from home, from airports, from hotel lobbies. An IT department cannot physically touch every device. So they use a management platform — a single pane of glass from which one administrator can see, control, and issue commands to every device in the organization, regardless of where on earth it happens to be.
Microsoft Intune is one of the most widely deployed of these platforms. From a single web-based console, one administrator can enforce security policies, push software updates, and remotely wipe a device if it is reported lost or stolen.
Or wipe all of them at once.
Think of it like a building where one master key opens every door — the server room, the vault, the executive offices, the parking garage — and that key hangs on a hook by the front desk, twenty-four hours a day. Nobody checks who picks it up. Nobody logs who used it. It just hangs there, fully loaded, because it makes things convenient for the staff. That is what Intune's administrative console represents. And when the wrong person picks up that key, every door in the building opens for them at once.
IT administrators will tell you that Intune can be maddeningly slow to push software updates across a large organization. But on the morning of this attack, the system worked with a speed it rarely manages under normal conditions — like a fire alarm that ignored every drill, then went off at full volume the one night it actually mattered. A single Bulk Wipe command moved through Microsoft's global cloud infrastructure and reached two hundred thousand devices in a matter of hours.
But here is the question that unlocks the whole story. How does an outsider get behind that front desk? How do they pick up the master key?

chapters.title: PART THREE — STEALING THE KEY
chapters.scriptText:
Most people understand basic phishing — a fake email, a fake login page, and a password stolen in seconds. Most people also understand that multi-factor authentication — the code sent to your phone — is supposed to stop this. And for a while, it did.
But attackers adapted. The technique is called Adversary-in-the-Middle — and it shatters the security most companies thought was unbeatable.
Think of it like a forger who doesn't copy your signature — they make you sign the real document yourself, then steal it out of your hand on the way to the bank. When you type your credentials into their fake login page, it invisibly forwards them to the real Microsoft server in real time. The real server asks for your multi-factor authentication code. The fake page asks you for it too. You enter it. It gets forwarded. The real server is satisfied — and issues an authenticated session token, a digital stamp that says this user has already been verified. The attacker captures it.
From the platform's perspective, attackers holding a valid session token were a legitimate administrator. They had always been a legitimate administrator. Nothing flagged the login as suspicious. Because technically, it wasn't.
[The score darkens.]
But how do you get to that moment? How do you target the right person — an administrator with Intune access — out of fifty-six thousand employees?
You don't find them overnight. You watch. You study. You wait.

chapters.title: PART FIVE — THE WIPER WITHOUT A WIPER
chapters.scriptText:
Here is the thing about sophisticated cyberattacks that most coverage gets wrong: the dramatic moment — the wipe, the dark screen — is almost never the beginning. It is the end.
The beginning is months earlier. Quiet. Patient. Invisible.
The group that claimed responsibility — Handala, an Iran-linked operation assessed by Palo Alto Networks as a front for the Iranian Ministry of Intelligence and Security — has a well-documented pattern. They do not rush. In previous intrusions studied by Check Point Research, Handala maintained quiet access inside target networks for months before executing a destructive phase. During that time, they did nothing that would trigger an alert. They simply watched.
But Handala is not a single actor. Forensic analysis by Check Point Research and Palo Alto Networks identifies a two-team model at the heart of these operations. The first team — tracked as Scarred Manticore — specialises in the slow work: long-dwell infiltration, credential harvesting, months of silent reconnaissance. They are the ones who get through the door and find the master key. The second team — Void Manticore, the group that operates the Handala persona — handles the destruction. They are brought in at the end, when everything is already in position, to press the button. Two separate groups. Two separate roles. One catastrophic outcome.
Based on Handala's documented history, their typical entry point is not glamorous: credential stuffing against corporate VPN infrastructure. Hundreds of failed login attempts, disguised behind generic hostnames. Most fail. Eventually, one doesn't. The timeline of the specific Stryker intrusion has not been publicly confirmed — but the pattern is consistent across every operation this group has been attributed to.
Once inside, they move laterally — spreading from one system to the next using the same remote access tools a helpdesk technician uses to fix a problem. To reach systems not directly accessible from the outside, they have been observed using open-source tunneling software that lets them build a private channel through the target's own infrastructure. Their weapons are the organization's own hallways.
What they are looking for is not a file or a server. It is an identity. A privileged identity — an account that holds administrative rights over something powerful. Something like Intune.
When they find it, they don't immediately use it. They confirm the access, test the permissions, and wait. Then they build the phishing relay, send the targeted email, capture the session token, and log in.
The preparation, based on this group's established pattern, took months. The attack itself took hours.

chapters.title: PART FIVE — THE WIPER WITHOUT A WIPER
chapters.scriptText:
What happened next has no real precedent in the history of corporate cyberattacks.
The attackers did not upload malware. They did not exploit a vulnerability in Windows or iOS. They did not write a single line of malicious code. They logged into the Intune console and issued one native administrative command: Bulk Wipe All Devices.
This is what security researchers call a Living-off-the-Land attack. Think of it like a burglar who breaks into your house, finds the spare key hanging in the kitchen, and uses it to walk back out the front door with everything you own. In broad daylight. No broken windows. No alarm. To every neighbor watching, it looks like you're moving house. To every security system monitoring Stryker's network, the command that erased two hundred thousand devices looked exactly like a legitimate instruction from a legitimate administrator. Because technically, it was. The administrator just happened to be a threat actor who had stolen the session token.
There was no malware signature to detect. No anomalous process to flag. No antivirus in the world would have stopped it, because there was nothing for antivirus to scan. The weapon was already inside, trusted and waiting.
The command reached laptops and tablets, servers and smartphones. And it reached the personal phones of thousands of employees who had enrolled their personal devices in the corporate management system to access work email.
This is where the attack crosses from corporate disaster into something more personal.
The wipe was a full device wipe — not a corporate data wipe. For employees with enrolled personal phones, it deleted everything. Family photographs. Videos of children's first steps. Wedding footage. It wiped eSIM profiles, leaving people unable to make calls or send texts. Employees whose phones went blank at three in the morning couldn't even call their families to say what was happening. They communicated through WhatsApp on old devices that had never been enrolled — because everything else had been turned to glass.
And here is a dimension that received almost no coverage: the legal exposure. When a company's device management system factory-resets an employee's personal property — destroying irreplaceable personal data — that is not a cybersecurity incident. That is the destruction of private property. The liability questions that follow are significant, and they remain largely unresolved.
No ransom demand was ever issued. This was not an attack designed to extract money. It was an attack designed to erase. That distinction matters — because it means there was no negotiation phase, no window in which the company might have minimized the damage. The destruction was the point. And it was total.

chapters.title: PART SIX — TOTAL DARKNESS
chapters.scriptText:
By sunrise, Stryker was in total darkness.
Manufacturing lines were motionless. Login screens displayed the attacker's logo where corporate interfaces used to be. Hospital supply chain managers across the United States were realizing that surgical equipment they had ordered was not coming — and making calls to emergency backup suppliers they hoped they would never need.
A healthcare professional at a major university medical center told journalists they were unable to order surgical supplies they normally sourced through Stryker. 'This is a real-world supply chain attack,' they said. 'Pretty much every hospital in the US that performs surgeries uses their supplies.'
But here is the detail that rarely made the headlines: the operating theatres kept running. Stryker's actual medical hardware — the Mako surgical robots, the LIFEPAK defibrillators, the Vocera clinical communication platforms — had been architecturally separated from the corporate Microsoft environment. They were air-gapped. The attackers had destroyed the company's entire digital infrastructure and could not touch a single device in a single operating room. Surgeons carried surgical plans on encrypted drives. Procedures continued. The corporate network was ash. The instruments were fine.
The LifeNet disruption was different. LifeNet is not a device — it is a transmission service, sitting in the layer between the paramedic in the ambulance and the physician waiting at the hospital. When hospitals began disconnecting from Stryker's systems as a precaution, that layer went dark. Paramedics could still read a patient's ECG. They just couldn't transmit it. They described the findings over radio instead. The device worked. The connection did not.
And the voicemail at Stryker's headquarters still said: 'We are currently experiencing a building emergency. Please try your call again later.'

chapters.title: PART SEVEN — REBUILDING FROM NOTHING
chapters.scriptText:
You cannot clean a computer that has been factory-reset. There is nothing to clean. The drive is gone. The operating system is gone. Everything is gone.
Restoring a wiped device is like rebuilding a house that dissolved overnight — brick by brick, from the foundation up, while the family stands on the lawn in their pajamas. Two hundred thousand houses. One night.
Every device must be physically collected, reimaged from scratch, tested, and returned. That means palletizing laptops and shipping them to regional imaging depots. Couriers. Freight. Customs paperwork for equipment that, forty-eight hours ago, was a functioning asset and is now dead weight. You cannot push a recovery script remotely — the management tool itself was the weapon. You cannot email instructions to employees — their email is gone. Every step of the recovery must be done manually, in person, at scale, across nearly eighty countries.
Security experts estimated full recovery at several months. During that time, Stryker's operations ran on emergency workarounds — pen and paper in facilities that hadn't used them in twenty years, phone calls in place of automated supply systems, manual orders in place of digital logistics.
A twenty-five-billion-dollar medical technology company — running on infrastructure from 1987. Because one session token was stolen through a phishing relay.

chapters.title: PART EIGHT — WHAT ACTUALLY FAILED
chapters.scriptText:
Once the shock subsided, the security community ran its post-mortem. And the conclusion was uncomfortable: the chaos had little to do with the sophistication of the attack. It had everything to do with an organizational failure to cope with a threat that should have been manageable.
The Stryker attack was not a brilliant zero-day exploit. There was no exotic malware, no cunning obfuscation. What it exploited was an architectural decision — that a single administrative account should hold permanent, always-on, global power over two hundred thousand devices.
One account. Two hundred thousand devices. No approval chain. No second signature. No expiration on the privilege.
The concept that stands against this is called Just-in-Time access. Under this model, administrators hold zero permissions by default. When they need to perform an elevated action — deploy a patch, run a diagnostic, wipe a device — they request that access through a formal workflow, for a specific task, for a specific window of time. The access is granted, the task is performed, the privilege expires. An attacker who steals your session token inherits only the permissions you held in that precise moment — not a standing, permanent, global kill-switch.
Microsoft's own identity platform offers exactly this capability. It is not a third-party add-on. It is a native feature of the same Microsoft ecosystem Stryker was already using. Whether it was implemented — and if not, why not — will be central to the post-incident analysis for years.
The second failure is almost embarrassingly simple: there was no multi-person authorization for a destructive command. Nuclear launch protocols require two officers to simultaneously turn their keys. Major financial transfers require dual authorization. The ability to simultaneously factory-reset every device in a fifty-six-thousand-person global company is — by any reasonable definition — catastrophic and irreversible. It should require more than one administrator to authorize. It should require a confirmation step that creates even a brief window for an alert system to fire.
It did not. Because convenience, accumulated across years of small decisions, had won out over friction. Because no one seriously imagined, until it happened, that this button would ever be pressed by the wrong hands.
The authentication failure is equally specific. Adversary-in-the-Middle phishing defeats standard multi-factor authentication — the industry has known this for years. The solution exists: hardware security keys are cryptographically bound to the legitimate domain. A relay cannot intercept them because there is nothing to intercept — the handshake between the key and the server cannot be faked or forwarded. They are called phishing-resistant for a reason. They are also the least deployed protection for the highest-privilege accounts, which is precisely where the stakes are greatest.
The tools to prevent this existed before it happened. Some were native features of platforms the organization already paid for. The gap was not technical. It was the accumulation of deferred decisions — and the universal human tendency to weigh convenience over the risk, to treat an unlikely disaster as someone else's problem. Until it isn't.

chapters.title: PART NINE — THE LESSON
chapters.scriptText:
History has a pattern here. When a major institution suffers a catastrophic breach, the post-mortem rarely concludes that the attacker was simply too clever to stop. More often, it concludes that the institution had been warned, had the tools, and had the resources — and made organizational choices that left a door open anyway. The breach is the moment the bill comes due.
The Stryker attack fits that pattern precisely. Not a failure of technology, but a failure of architecture — of the institutional decisions that govern how much power a single credential is allowed to hold, how that credential is protected, and what safeguards exist against its misuse.
Every organization that uses a modern device management platform has built a self-destruct button into its own infrastructure. It exists for legitimate reasons — lost devices need to be wiped, stolen laptops need to be cleared. The capability is necessary. The question is who holds the key, how many people can use it, and what an attacker needs to steal in order to press it themselves.
If the answer is a single session token and a web browser — the button is already armed.

chapters.title: EPILOGUE — THE GLOW, REVISITED
chapters.scriptText:
The phone on the nightstand is still dark. Sometime in the night, the employee let go of the idea of recovering it. The photos are gone. The videos are gone. The small, irreplaceable record of a life. All of it gone — erased by a command that looked, to every system that processed it, like a routine administrative action.
In the next room, the laptop sits open, its power light blinking slowly. A technician will come today to collect it. Somewhere, it will join a pallet of machines being shipped to a regional imaging center — one laptop among two hundred thousand. Eventually it will come back, blank and clean, and need to be rebuilt from nothing.
In corporate IT offices around the world this morning, administrators are looking at their own consoles with slightly different eyes. Looking at the wipe command. Looking at who else has access to it. Asking questions they probably should have asked before.
The self-destruct button has always been there. We built it ourselves, for good reasons, with the best of intentions. The only thing that changed on March 11, 2026 is that now we know — with certainty — exactly how easy it is for someone else to press it.
